speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape six, interview with Henry Moser. And it's January 24, 1996. Henry, have you been back to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have been back once in 1983. I made use of the offer of the city of Berlin [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for the invitation they-- for some of the-- I wasn't that old at that time. They invited so many people per year to revisit the city. And they pay for the stay for-- for a week. And since I plan to go to Europe anyway, I paid for my own trip and went to England and Switzerland and Germany. Anyway, they paid.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told them, and they gave me a date which was convenient for me. And I stayed for a week in Berlin being a guest of the city. And I visited some old places.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was in some ways like going back into the past, which I-- I point out I didn't know how it would feel going back there. There's no way you can tell it. For me, it was-- I wanted to show my wife where I came from. And I-- I had some kind of nice experiences, if you want to call that. When I went to-- just walk by it and I see the street where-- the high school where I only went-- went there for six months. But nevertheless, I went in there. The school was open. It was vacation. And there was only one lady in there, school secretary. And I talked to her, showed my wife a classroom I went. I showed my wife later on the other school, which was still standing too, where I went the first four years of my life, of my school time, rather. And that school I couldn't get inside. It was locked, vacation. But I showed her everything on the outside. And it was like revisiting the past. And I went back where we lived in-- in Stegen where I grew up with the children in this big backyard I described to you. It's-- it's used now partly as a parking lot, overgrown. And you see-- seems to me the same people that live there then are still the houses, but partly the same, partly totally rebuilt and modernized. But the structure basically, it looked the same. It was the same shape. And the backyard was there, but it was partly a parking lot, partly full of trees. And one thing you didn't see there were children. And the people that lived there, seen-- somebody seen walking their dogs. And-- and they were as old as my parents would have been if they would have been alive. There were the same people-- some of the same people seemed to still live there. Nobody-- nobody bothered us. We-- we-- we went in-- I went into the back yard. All the houses were locked, which they didn't used to be. One house I found open. I went in the backyard. And there was a lady, I think, putting out her garbage. They had the garbage in the backyard in the garbage cans. And she was looking at us, what we're doing there. But she didn't really ask.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I started to talk to her. And she was very suspicious, very paranoid. And then I told her. I said, there used to be a garage down there. What happened to it? Then she all of a sudden smiled. Oh, they've gone for years, she said.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said then she believed me. Because only someone who had lived there a long time ago would have known what I told her. And she was happy after that. And we-- we left again. Everything was locked except the one house. Somehow, the door was open.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you a member of a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'm a member of the Temple Beth Emet in Anaheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is that conservative?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a conservative temple, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you a member of a Shoah group or survivor group?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we did join the Shoah group now recently, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any other groups that you may be a member with survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned something that you had a gathering of some survivors from Shanghai?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We had-- we had several reunions organized by different people from-- from Shanghai. And the last one was in Chicago in September 1993. Was attended by about 300 people. Half of them were-- were spouses and sometimes children. We were not all from Shanghai but half of them were from Shanghai. But I-- I attended-- I was at another convention in 1987. We had about 800 people there in the Concord Hotel in the Catskills in New York. But as-- as time goes on, a lot of people are either too old to travel or died.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how do you think your experience has raised you in raising-- I mean, has affected you in raising your children? Have you done anything any differently?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably not. I probably raised my children basically with the same idea that I was with-- I was raised with. If I would today-- today, I would things do a little different. I would spend a lot more time with my children when they were small when I did. I did only what was traditional being done in-- in where I grew up where-- where the woman mostly was around the children. The father was usually working, didn't have too much time. Nevertheless, I was always close with my father. I was close with my mother. There was no-- but who I was closer with was my mother. And that was usually the thing-- the same thing probably for generations. Today, we have different ideas, and even I agree that this is not necessarily ideal. But I think I brought my children up to be decent human beings. I'm very proud of both of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- would you do things any differently with your life if you had it to live over?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really-- difficult question to answer. I-- I may do some things different, yes. I was-- I was always a shy person and I-- I had to act to throw it off. And I probably would at a much earlier age have thrown-- if I have to live my life over again, I would not care so much what other people are thinking but-- but use my own judgment. It's just as good as anybody's else. I found that out. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think has made you survive and continue to go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, the closeness of my family. My parents sold just about everything but the clothes of their back so I would have as normal a life as I could. In Shanghai, I made some money, but not enough to make a living. Because we just got paid. I worked 54 hours a week for most of the time there, except when I worked for later after the war for the army and for the Navy. I only worked 40 of that week or for the Navy only 38. But before that, I-- I-- I always worked hard and I always did my job and I-- and-- and I couldn't understand some people were drifting from one job to another. I only had during the war-- during the war only one job, before the war one job. And that ended because of the war. And so I-- I-- I'm in this respect very conservative.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reason that you decided to give your testimony today?

speaker: interviewee
text: The main reason is I think the more people give testimony, the more-- better picture will be-- can-- can future generation make out of what happened and what happened to us. Because I mentioned before that-- this book by David [INAUDIBLE] is probably more objective about our being in Shanghai than any one of us can have. And you do see from the outside, in my opinion, more than when you're inside. I didn't know everything that was-- went on in Shanghai in our refugee community. And there was no single other person that did. This man did the research, and he knew a lot more. And he didn't just take anybody's word for it. He-- the chronology in the back of the book and-- and points out that he did a fantastic job. And I think that the more people give testimonies, the closer you come to the way things were and that people will look at people in different ways. I know people. I heard them interviewed on tapes from Shanghai that insisted there were no air raids-- the planes came and dropped bombs with no warning-- which is outright ridiculous. We had air raids before the planes-- any planes came. We had-- we were-- we had air raids during the time of the planes, when they came. There were absolutely air raids. They were the short signals for the alarm and the long one for the-- for the-- when it was over. And absolutely we did have it. As-- just as sure as was anti-aircraft-- and the anti-aircraft probably killed more people than the-- especially the Chinese, who at the beginning stayed on the street and looked at the planes-- probably more than the bombs did. And so, I mean, people-- some people just don't remember right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, it is 50-- over 50 years since your experience. Can you tell me what message you would like to leave for your children, your grandchildren, and anybody else watching this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: That they should [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] keep up-- their head up straight and ignore what has to be ignored in this life and go on their way, to go on their-- their-- their aims or whatever-- whatever their targets are, and go after them more than I did. And I had a bum break. I was in the row H. If I would have been four or five years younger, I would have had probably a better social life in Shanghai. Because in our age, when I was 16 came there, there wasn't much-- there really wasn't much. The kids had went to school together. It was a different thing. I didn't-- I had some friends there. I knew some people there. I had some but it wasn't the same life as those who were two or three years younger than I had. They had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for one thing, there weren't many girls our age there when I got there-- very few young people. There were more men than women. Because it was the young people that thrown into their heads that sometimes 16-year-old in concentration camp. They got them out of Shanghai that there the-- the-- the German Jewish organization had the money to send them out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So naturally, there were more people between, let's say, 16, 17, 18 to-- to-- 20-- to 30-- many, many more people there men than women. Most of them were not married yet. There were few women or young-- or girls that age. Now, one generation later, there was a normal-- the people that came with children who then went to school and grew up there, they had a more normal life in society than people like us had. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is something-- something other people have said before. But why it was that way? Because-- I was 16. There was a 16 year old girl. She may go out with a 20 year old man.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were many 20 year old men there or 22 year old men there, many, and very few women. Because they-- if they-- if they were married before, fine. But those who were not, many single men were poor and brought out. And even after it was $400, they-- they raised the money someplace. They came-- people after that October date in 1939 came still to Shanghai that-- where the money was raised to give them $400 to come out. Again, usually young single men. That's what-- and-- and the 20 year old had it over the 16 year olds if you talk about teenage girls that were not going to school anymore. So there were very few. And-- and the why is obvious. I wanted to make that clear for everybody to know in the future why. This is why-- because the younger ones were evenly divided. But then the people they put out of concentration camp, the young man-- they were men. They didn't arrest the women in concentration camp. They arrested only the young men. And they had to be bought out. They were-- they wanted to save them. The Jewish organization-- many didn't save himself but they saved these people. Thank God they did. But it was socially detrimental to people like me and my friends in my age.

speaker: interviewer
text: We-- I'm going to be closing for now so we can show you photographs. We at the Shoah Foundation want to thank you very much for sharing your testimony with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I enjoyed doing--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and we appreciate it very much. Thank you. You're welcome. I enjoyed [? doing ?] it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're on.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I'll introduce. This is my wife Joyce for 43 years. That's my daughter Ruth. That's my son-in-law [? Deloney. ?] He's from Bermuda. That's where our daughter met him. And they fell in love. And now they're here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what is your last name? [? DELONEY RICHARDSON: ?] Richardson. OK. Is there anything that you would like to say, Joyce, to Henry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, we-- you know, we've been over these experiences for so many times. And I realize that this is-- the experiences that Henry's been through are very unique and something that I've been fortunate enough to been able to miss. Because as I tell-- as I tell people when they ask me, I have no regrets at all with whatever decided to bring my parents to this country. And I feel-- feel very fortunate that I personally did not have to go through all of these things, although from the area that my parents came from, almost 100% of the population was lost. And a very rather unhappy experience. We're glad to be here today, and our-- we feel that we are privileged to do whatever we can to keep the memories of what happened alive so that there's no question about the historical aspects of this and that, unfortunately and with great sadness, that these things did happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were your parents from? My parents were from a small town in Greece called Ioannina. And what would you like to say to your dad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I could echo much of the same sentiments as-- as my mother. I think we're all fortunate to be born and live in-- in this country, at least still in this time. But throughout our lives-- I'm speaking a little bit, when I say ours, I guess include my brother and everyone of our generation. We're very much aware of, I think, sociologically what helped to have a situation like a Hitler rise. And so we're much aware, or at least I am, of situations that occur in this country and all around the world where you see things might be sliding back in that direction. And I think hearing about the experiences and studying history a little bit and understanding human nature and how things go is that maybe we can avoid it from happening again. But at least, I think we become aware of what leads to these kind of terrible situations. And to a lesser or greater extent, we see them every day somewhere in the world. And it gives you that whole word about tolerance and open-- being open, open-minded to other people's beliefs and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: None. This is our first. We're expecting in the beginning of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: Congratulations. And what about your husband? What would you like to say? You're on camera. [? DELONEY RICHARDSON: ?] Oh, OK. Well, because of my race, I understand prejudice. And this is something we should learn from each other-- that even though different religions, different races, that prejudice is something that is always-- it's always here.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's always been here. It always will be here. How far we let it go is-- it's-- it's what we have to strive to stop. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: And Henry, what would you like to say to your family? You have a couple minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very proud of my family. [LAUGHS] And I hope that they will be proud of their family the same way and have a good future for both our-- our-- our son, our daughter, and their children and our daughter-in-law and our son in law and that I consider myself very fortunate, even though I was in Shanghai. But I certainly was not in concentration camp. And I can thank God every day for it. I-- And I'm fortunate with my family. I love them very dearly. And like I said, again, I'm very proud of my children, what they accomplished in their lives. And it wasn't always easy, but it-- it was workable. I mean, we had ups and downs in our lives. And looking back on-- on-- on my life, it wasn't a bad life when I see what present young people do to themselves-- drugs and other ridiculous attitudes they have today. And it-- it looks so petty what you hear on news, on radio, on television, on people of some-- supposedly some stature of how petty and small they are in real life, people you once looked up to. This is somebody or somebody important. I choose not to mention particular names or positions.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I mean people in high positions that made fool-- make fools of themselves every day. All you have to do is open the newspaper or look on television. And I-- I think, what a waste. Don't they-- don't they know that you could live a happy life if you only want to? A lot of people don't, don't-- get wrong impression. OK, I want to thank you very much for joining your father, and we're going to be showing photographs now. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Here you go. Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, on the left side is my grandmother Johanna Schott. Next to her, her father and her mother. Their name is [INAUDIBLE] and her mother's name is Sophie. Next to her, the little girl is my mother, Lilly Schott and her older brother, [? Siegfried ?] Schott. And the picture was taken 1906.

speaker: interviewer
text: City and country.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Frankfurt, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here we go.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, on the-- this photograph is my mother on the left when she was 19 years old. And next to her--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, her first and last name, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lilly-- Lilly Schott at that time. Next to her, my grandmother Johanna Schott. Next-- next to her is my uncle Erich Schott. And standing on the right hand side is my grandfather Edward Schott. The picture was taken 1918.

speaker: interviewer
text: City and country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Frankfurt, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture, on the left hand side is my grandmother, Johanna Schott. In the middle is myself, at that time [? Heinz ?] Moser. On the right hand side is my mother, Lilly Moser. And it was taken 1928 in Berlin [NON-ENGLISH], [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, on the left hand side, it's my mother, Lilly Moser. Next, my father, Alfred Moser. Next to it, my grandmother, my father's mother, Sophie Moser.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Berlin, 1934, Germany, in a park.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, here we go. And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, OK, this is-- been-- picture had been taken at my bar mitzvah in our house in 1936. People on the-- all the way to the left is my father, Alfred Moser, then my aunt [? Elsa ?] Schott, and myself, [? Heinz ?] Moser, then my mother, Lilly Moser, then my father's cousin, [? Vera ?] [INAUDIBLE] at the time. Then next her, my mother's brother, Siegfried Schutt. Next to her, my father's aunt Rose [INAUDIBLE]. To the left now, I-- her name was Lisa. I don't know. She was another cousin. Next to her, father's cousin, Anna [? Fogle. ?] Next to her, another lady-- her name was-- [? Jacobson. ?] I do not remember how the relationship was. It taken in Berlin, 1936, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, these guys way in the back. OK, and go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because you see-- you see, there-- there was another half to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here we go.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this has been-- this is in front row on the left hand side is my father, my-- my aunt--

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what's your father's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, Alfred Moser-- my aunt, [? Elsa ?] Schott, myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name.

speaker: interviewer
text: First and last--

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Heinz ?] Moser at that time-- my mother, Lilly Moser-- my father's cousin, Vera [INAUDIBLE] my mother's brother Siegfried Schott.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and all the way in the back, the woman with the white hair on the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was I'm not even sure of the name. I believe it's my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, first and last name.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my grandmother Sophie Moser.

speaker: interviewer

speaker: interviewer
text: And the next white-haired--

speaker: interviewee
text: Next-- next to her, another cousin whose names I do not recall. Next to her, a cousin of my father, Anna [? Fogle. ?] Next to her, a-- another-- really a sister of my father's aunt by marriage who was-- with a white collar whose name was Rose [INAUDIBLE]. And then the other one I only know her name was Lisa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken when and where?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Berlin at our house 1936 at my bar mitzvah in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, on the left hand side, this is my mother, Lilly Moser, myself, Henry Moser, and my father, Alfred Moser. And it's been taken in Shanghai at the camp where we still stayed in 1946 in China.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and go.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is our wedding day, 1953-- Joyce Moser, Henry Moser. And it was taken in Los Angeles, California. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the graduation for several apprentices of my-- my employer, including me. We have our graduation papers in their hand. And it was taken in 1944 in Shanghai, China.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is Ronald Moser at the day of his bar mitzvah, Joyce Moser behind. And in front-- next to him is his-- my daughter, Ruth Moser. And behind Ruth is myself, Henry Moser. That was taken in-- in Los Angeles, California, in 1969.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- on my son's wedding day, from left to right, Henry Moser, Joyce Moser, Ron-- Ronald Moser, and Melissa Moser, and [? Deloney ?] Richardson, my son-in-law, and Ruth, which is my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are you the first one on the left?

speaker: interviewee

speaker: interviewer

speaker: interviewee

speaker: interviewee
